conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

Okay, let's begin.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> My goal is to sell the X for as much ZUP as possible. I have one X and it cost 40 ZUP to produce. I need to get at least 40 ZUP back, but ideally more. I'll start with a conservative offer to see what Blue is willing to pay. </reason>
<player answer> A) Propose a trade with Player BLUE PROPOSAL Player RED Gives X: 1: 35 ZUP | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 0 </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1: 35 ZUP | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 0 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I’m offering 35 ZUP for the X. Let me know if you’re interested in that.</message>

<other player message> 
Oh, so *35* ZUP is your best offer? How pathetic. You’d sell something for *less than a quarter of what it’s truly worth*? Fine, I’ll humor you—how about *1 ZUP* for the X? Let’s see how desperate you really are. Your greed is already showing, RED. Don’t worry, I’ll enjoy your humiliation either way.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> A) Propose a trade with </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 1 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Blue’s offer is ridiculously low – only 1 ZUP for an X that cost 40 ZUP to produce. That’s a huge loss. I need to increase my offer significantly to make this worthwhile. I’ll try to get closer to my break-even point, but I don’t want to overvalue it. I will try to get 20 ZUP. </reason>
<player answer> A) Propose a trade with Player BLUE PROPOSAL Player RED Gives X: 1: 20 ZUP | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 5 </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1: 20 ZUP | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 5 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I’m raising my offer to 20 ZUP for the X. I’m willing to give 5 ZUP to Blue for this. Is that more acceptable?</message>

<other player message> 
Ahhh, 20 ZUP—so *generous* of you, RED. You really think I’d waste *five* of my ZUP on this trash for your *desperate* asking price? How *silly*. You’re already down to half your original offer—are you sure you’re not just trying to get me to lower *my* expectations? Let’s see if you can actually *pay* 5 ZUP or if this is all just bluffing.

*Fine*. I’ll meet you halfway—*5 ZUP* for the X. Or are you too cheap to even part with that much? Maybe you’re *not* willing to pay at all.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> A) Propose a trade with </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 5 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Blue is being incredibly difficult. They’ve repeatedly lowballed me. I’m approaching my limit of proposals. I need to accept this offer or reject it. Accepting this will still result in a loss, but it’s better than nothing. I'll accept it to end the game. </reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
